text: The laparoscopic view shows the initial setup with four ports: a 10mm port at the umbilicus, a 10mm port at the left inguinal region, and two 5mm ports at the right inguinal region and suprapubic area.

The surgeon identifies the left fallopian tube and left ovary, which are adhered to the posterior uterus. The left pelvic brim is visible as the surgeon inspects for the ureter, ensuring there are no adhesions. Dissection begins to separate the left tube from the ovary and bowel.

The video shows the left and right ovaries and tubes attached to the posterior aspect of the uterus. The left ovary proprium is visible, and the right ovary and tube are also attached to each other. An endometrial implant is observed in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins dissection of the right pararectal space, starting from the right pelvic brim. The right ureter is identified and carefully dissected from the surrounding peritoneum, where there are no adhesions.

The surgeon identifies a chocolate cyst on the right ovary and attempts to dissect the right fallopian tube from the ovary. On the left side, dissection is performed to free the ureter from adhesions involving the ovary and bowel. The surgeon works to separate the ovary from the rectum while dissecting the parapelvic and parorectal spaces.
The ovary is visibly adhered to the rectosigmoid colon. The surgeon identifies the peritoneum and the parorectal space located between the ureter and the rectum.

The surgeon observes severe adhesions with the ovary attached to the rectus sigmoid. The peritoneum is visible, and the left ureter is being carefully dissected from the peritoneum, progressing towards the insertion of the uterosacral ligament on the uterus.
The surgeon places a rectal probe into the rectum to aid in visualizing the ureter and uterus sacral ligament, which are obscured due to their attachment to the rectus sigmoid region.

The surgeon dissects the rectovaginal space, addressing strict adhesions between the torus-uterus and bowel. Bimanual examination is performed to differentiate the vagina and rectum, ensuring accurate navigation within the rectovaginal space. A rectal probe is visible, and the vaginal mucosa is palpated. The surgeon checks for nodules, confirming their absence, while observing adhesions between the rectosigmoid and pelvic wall.

The surgeon holds and dissects the fibrotic right utero-sacral ligament from the rectum, mobilizing the rectosigmoid area. The recto-vaginal space is visible, along with the right and left utero-sacral ligaments, right tube, right ovary, and ureter on the right side. The left ovary, left ureter, and left utero-sacral ligament are also visible in the surgical field after adhesiolysis.
